past_medical_history: Hypertension, diagnosed 18 years ago, on multiple agents
Type 2 diabetes mellitus, diagnosed 10 years ago
Mild aortic stenosis noted on echocardiogram 2 years ago
Prior hospitalization for chest pain 3 years ago, cardiac catheterization showed non-obstructive coronary artery disease
family_history: Father had a pacemaker placed at age 71 for 'slow heart rate,' died at 79 of heart failure
Mother had hypertension and type 2 diabetes, died at 74 of stroke
Older brother has atrial fibrillation, on warfarin
No family history of sudden cardiac death or seizure disorders

social_history.tobacco: Former smoker, 15-pack-year history, quit 12 years ago
social_history.occupation: Bus driver for the city transit authority, currently on medical leave
social_history.living_situation: Lives with wife in a two-story townhome
social_history.exercise: Previously walked 30 minutes daily, has curtailed activity over past 2 months due to fear of passing out

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert and oriented to person, place, time, and situation. Glasgow Coma Scale 15. Speech fluent without dysarthria or aphasia. Attention intact, able to perform serial sevens accurately. Recall 3/3 objects at 5 minutes. No confusion. No post-ictal features observed at time of evaluation, approximately 45 minutes after the most recent episode.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: CN II: Visual fields full to confrontation bilaterally. Pupils equal, round, reactive to light 3mm to 2mm bilaterally. Fundoscopic exam reveals mild arteriolar narrowing bilaterally consistent with chronic hypertension, no papilledema. CN III/IV/VI: Extraocular movements intact, no nystagmus. CN V: Sensation intact in all three divisions bilaterally, masseter strength symmetric. CN VII: Face symmetric at rest and with activation. CN VIII: Hearing intact to finger rub bilaterally. CN IX/X: Palate elevates symmetrically, uvula midline, gag reflex intact bilaterally. CN XI: Sternocleidomastoid and trapezius strength 5/5 bilaterally. CN XII: Tongue midline without fasciculations or atrophy.
neurological_exam.motor: Bulk normal. Tone normal, no rigidity or spasticity. Strength 5/5 throughout in all major muscle groups bilaterally including deltoids, biceps, triceps, wrist extensors, finger abductors, hip flexors, quadriceps, hamstrings, tibialis anterior, and gastrocnemius. No pronator drift. No tremor.
neurological_exam.sensory: Light touch, pinprick, vibration, and proprioception intact in upper extremities. Mildly decreased vibration sense at bilateral great toes. Proprioception intact distally. No dermatomal sensory loss.
neurological_exam.reflexes: Biceps 2+ bilaterally, triceps 2+ bilaterally, brachioradialis 2+ bilaterally, patellar 2+ bilaterally, Achilles 1+ bilaterally. Plantar responses flexor (downgoing) bilaterally. No clonus.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose testing normal bilaterally without dysmetria or intention tremor. Heel-to-shin testing normal bilaterally. Rapid alternating movements intact and symmetric.
neurological_exam.gait: Steady gait with normal base and stride length. Tandem gait intact. Romberg negative.
neurological_exam.additional: No tongue laceration or evidence of oral trauma. No evidence of urinary incontinence. Small ecchymosis on left temporal region from fall during most recent episode. No signs of prolonged convulsive activity.

patient.chief_complaint: Recurrent episodes of sudden loss of consciousness with witnessed shaking movements
patient.history_present_illness: Mr. Raymond Okafor is a 62-year-old man with a history of hypertension on multiple medications, type 2 diabetes, hyperlipidemia, and obstructive sleep apnea who presents to the emergency department after a third episode of sudden loss of consciousness in six weeks. Today's episode occurred while he was sitting in a chair watching television at approximately 2:30 PM. His wife, who was in the room, reports that he suddenly became unresponsive and slumped to the left, falling out of the chair onto the floor. She observed brief jerking movements of both arms and legs lasting approximately 10 to 15 seconds. She describes his face as looking ashen gray during the episode. She called 911 and by the time she returned to his side approximately 30 seconds later, he was already waking up and asking what happened. She states he was fully alert and conversational within one minute of the event. There was no preceding cry, no tonic stiffening, no eye deviation, and no frothing at the mouth. She did not notice any tongue biting or urinary incontinence. The previous two episodes occurred three weeks ago and five weeks ago, respectively. The first episode happened while he was standing in line at the grocery store and was initially attributed to 'orthostatic hypotension' by his primary care physician, who reduced his hydrochlorothiazide dose. The second episode occurred while seated at the dinner table, and his wife again witnessed brief limb jerking for several seconds. He was referred to neurology for concern of new-onset seizures but the appointment was not for another two weeks. He has also noticed intermittent lightheadedness and a sensation of his heart 'pausing' or 'skipping' over the past two months, particularly when climbing stairs. He denies chest pain, sustained palpitations, shortness of breath at rest, or exertional dyspnea beyond his baseline. He denies any prior seizure history, head trauma, or family history of epilepsy. He has no history of aura, tongue biting, prolonged confusion, or prolonged shaking episodes.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 10 Hz alpha rhythm, symmetric, well-modulated, reactive to eye opening and closure
background.anterior_rhythms: Low-to-medium amplitude beta activity, symmetric, no focal slowing
background.sleep_features: Drowsiness captured with symmetric vertex waves and sleep spindles. Normal stage II sleep architecture.

artifacts.description: Intermittent low-amplitude muscle artifact, does not obscure underlying cerebral activity
ECG artifact noted intermittently; notable for irregular R-R intervals suggesting possible conduction abnormality. Recommend dedicated cardiac evaluation.

activating_procedures.hyperventilation: Performed for 3 minutes. Produced mild symmetric slowing that resolved within 30 seconds of cessation. No epileptiform discharges provoked.
activating_procedures.photic_stimulation: Performed at standard flash frequencies from 1 to 30 Hz. Normal photic driving response at 12-18 Hz. No photoparoxysmal response elicited.

eeg.impression: Normal awake and asleep EEG. No epileptiform discharges, no seizures captured during this 45-minute routine recording. The normal interictal EEG does not exclude a diagnosis of epilepsy but provides no electroencephalographic support for it. Of note, ECG artifact during recording demonstrated irregular R-R intervals; clinical correlation and dedicated cardiac monitoring are recommended.
eeg.limitations: Routine 45-minute recording. Sensitivity of a single routine interictal EEG for detecting epileptiform abnormalities is approximately 50%. Prolonged monitoring would increase sensitivity if clinical suspicion remains.
recommended_actions: If clinical suspicion for seizures persists, consider prolonged ambulatory EEG monitoring
Recommend dedicated cardiac evaluation given ECG artifact findings
Clinical correlation with cardiac monitoring results

ecg.rhythm: Sinus rhythm with bifascicular block (right bundle branch block and left anterior fascicular block)
findings: Sinus bradycardia at 54 bpm
First-degree AV block (PR interval 224 ms)
Right bundle branch block (rSR' pattern in V1-V2, wide S wave in I and V6)
Left anterior fascicular block (left axis deviation beyond -45 degrees)
Borderline QTc prolongation at 486 ms
Occasional premature ventricular complexes, 2 noted during recording
No ST elevation or acute ischemic changes
ecg.interpretation: Sinus bradycardia with trifascicular disease pattern: first-degree AV block combined with bifascicular block (RBBB plus left anterior fascicular block). This represents significant conduction system disease with only the left posterior fascicle still conducting. The combination of syncope with trifascicular disease pattern on ECG is a high-risk finding suggesting intermittent complete heart block as the likely mechanism for syncopal episodes, even though the current tracing does not capture the arrhythmia. Borderline QTc prolongation is also noted. Urgent cardiology consultation recommended.
ecg.clinical_correlation: The trifascicular conduction disease pattern (first-degree AV block plus bifascicular block) in a patient with recurrent syncope is highly suggestive of intermittent high-degree or complete AV block as the mechanism. The syncope likely occurs during transient progression to complete heart block with inadequate escape rhythm. This is an indication for hospital admission with continuous monitoring and likely permanent pacemaker implantation.

BMP.clinical_significance: Mildly low, likely related to hydrochlorothiazide use. Hypokalemia can exacerbate conduction abnormalities and prolong QTc.
Mildly elevated, likely prerenal component from thiazide diuretic use
Elevated, consistent with known diabetes mellitus; non-fasting specimen

Cardiac_markers.clinical_significance: Normal, no evidence of acute myocardial injury
Mildly elevated, may reflect subclinical cardiac dysfunction or hypertensive heart disease

Metabolic.clinical_significance: Mildly low, likely related to thiazide diuretic. Hypomagnesemia can contribute to cardiac arrhythmias and QTc prolongation.
Indicates suboptimally controlled diabetes, contributes to microvascular disease

labs.interpretation: Laboratory results show no acute metabolic derangement sufficient to cause syncope. Mild hypokalemia (3.4 mEq/L) and hypomagnesemia (1.6 mg/dL), both likely related to chronic hydrochlorothiazide use, are notable as they may exacerbate cardiac conduction abnormalities and contribute to QTc prolongation. BNP is mildly elevated at 178 pg/mL suggesting subclinical cardiac dysfunction. Troponin is normal, ruling out acute myocardial infarction. TSH is normal. Glucose and HbA1c reflect suboptimally controlled diabetes.

Echocardiogram.clinical_significance: Mildly reduced systolic function, likely related to chronic hypertensive heart disease
Concentric left ventricular hypertrophy consistent with chronic hypertension

Echocardiogram.value: Mild aortic stenosis with peak gradient 28 mmHg, mean gradient 15 mmHg, valve area 1.5 cm2
Echocardiogram.clinical_significance: Mild aortic stenosis with calcification; calcific degeneration may extend to conduction system

output.interpretation: Transthoracic echocardiogram demonstrates concentric left ventricular hypertrophy consistent with chronic hypertensive heart disease, with mildly reduced LVEF at 50%. Mild aortic stenosis with aortic valve calcification is noted. The calcific aortic valve disease may be contributing to progressive conduction system degeneration, which is consistent with the patient's bifascicular block and clinical presentation. Mild left atrial dilation is present. No significant mitral regurgitation. No pericardial effusion or intracardiac thrombus.
abnormal_values_summary: Mild aortic stenosis with valve calcification

output.rhythm: Predominantly sinus rhythm with bifascicular block; intermittent Mobitz type II second-degree AV block and one episode of transient complete heart block
findings: Baseline sinus rhythm with bifascicular block (RBBB + LAFB) and first-degree AV block throughout most of recording
Average heart rate 56 bpm (range 38-82 bpm)
Three episodes of Mobitz type II second-degree AV block with 2:1 conduction, each lasting 15-45 seconds
One episode of transient complete heart block at 19:42 lasting 8 seconds with ventricular escape at 32 bpm, coinciding with patient diary entry of 'felt dizzy, nearly passed out'
Maximum RR pause of 3.8 seconds during transient complete heart block
Isolated PVCs: 187 total in 24 hours (0.2% burden), no couplets or sustained ventricular tachycardia
Minimum heart rate 38 bpm at 04:12 during sleep, sinus bradycardia with persistent bifascicular block
No supraventricular tachycardia
output.interpretation: 48-hour Holter monitoring demonstrates progressive conduction system disease with baseline trifascicular block pattern (first-degree AV block plus bifascicular block), intermittent Mobitz type II second-degree AV block, and one documented episode of transient complete heart block with symptomatic near-syncope. The 3.8-second pause during transient complete heart block correlates with patient symptoms and is consistent with the mechanism of his syncopal episodes. This confirms intermittent high-degree AV block as the etiology of syncope.
output.clinical_correlation: The documentation of transient complete heart block with symptomatic near-syncope on Holter confirms the diagnosis of cardiac syncope due to progressive conduction system disease. The combination of trifascicular block pattern with intermittent progression to higher-degree block constitutes a Class I indication for permanent pacemaker implantation. Dual-chamber (DDD) pacemaker is recommended.

Tilt_table_test.clinical_significance: Modest drop but within acceptable range
Blunted chronotropic response due to conduction system disease

Tilt_table_test.value: Mild lightheadedness at 15 minutes, resolved spontaneously
Tilt_table_test.clinical_significance: Nonspecific, did not reproduce syncopal episodes

output.interpretation: Tilt table test is negative for a vasovagal mechanism. There is no significant orthostatic hypotension. Mild lightheadedness at 15 minutes did not reproduce the patient's typical syncopal episodes. The blunted heart rate response to tilt is consistent with underlying conduction system disease. This result does not support vasovagal or orthostatic syncope as the etiology and redirects attention to the cardiac conduction abnormalities.

output.rhythm: Sinus rhythm with bifascicular block at baseline; Mobitz type II block provoked during recovery phase
findings: Resting ECG shows sinus bradycardia with trifascicular block pattern
Achieved 5.2 METs, completed Stage 2 of modified Bruce protocol
Peak heart rate 98 bpm (62% of age-predicted maximum, severely blunted chronotropic response)
No exercise-induced ST changes suggestive of ischemia
Bifascicular block persisted throughout exercise
During recovery phase at 3 minutes post-exercise: two episodes of Mobitz type II second-degree AV block with 3:1 conduction, each lasting 5-8 seconds
Blood pressure response appropriate: rose from 148/88 to 186/92 at peak exercise
No ventricular tachycardia during exercise or recovery
output.interpretation: Exercise stress test demonstrates severely blunted chronotropic response with a peak heart rate of only 98 bpm (62% of age-predicted maximum). Importantly, Mobitz type II second-degree AV block with high-degree conduction ratios was provoked during the recovery phase, which is an ominous finding indicating unstable infranodal conduction. No exercise-induced ischemia. The provocation of higher-degree AV block during post-exercise vagal rebound strongly supports the diagnosis of intermittent high-degree AV block as the mechanism for this patient's syncope.
output.clinical_correlation: Exercise-provoked high-degree AV block during recovery is a well-recognized finding in patients with infranodal conduction disease and constitutes an additional indication for permanent pacemaker implantation. The severely blunted chronotropic response further supports significant conduction system disease.

signal_characteristics.T1: Mildly elevated native T1 values diffusely (1080 ms at 1.5T), suggestive of diffuse interstitial fibrosis
signal_characteristics.late_gadolinium_enhancement: Patchy mid-wall enhancement at the basal interventricular septum and inferior insertion points

signal_characteristics.cine: Restricted aortic valve opening consistent with mild stenosis
signal_characteristics.flow_analysis: Peak velocity 2.8 m/s across aortic valve

additional_observations: No evidence of arrhythmogenic right ventricular cardiomyopathy
No evidence of amyloidosis (no diffuse subendocardial LGE pattern)
output.impression: Cardiac MRI demonstrates concentric left ventricular hypertrophy with diffuse interstitial fibrosis on T1 mapping, consistent with hypertensive heart disease. Patchy mid-wall late gadolinium enhancement at the basal septum is in the region of the proximal conduction system (bundle of His) and may represent the structural substrate for the patient's progressive conduction disease. Aortic valve calcification with mild stenosis is confirmed. No evidence of infiltrative cardiomyopathy, sarcoidosis, or ARVC. The imaging findings support a diagnosis of degenerative conduction system disease related to chronic hypertensive remodeling and calcific aortic valve disease.
recommended_actions: Permanent pacemaker implantation for symptomatic high-degree AV block
Follow-up echocardiography in 6-12 months to monitor aortic stenosis progression

differential_by_imaging.diagnosis: Hypertensive heart disease with degenerative conduction system fibrosis
differential_by_imaging.rationale: Concentric LVH with diffuse interstitial fibrosis and basal septal mid-wall enhancement in region of conduction system

differential_by_imaging.diagnosis: Calcific conduction system disease from aortic valve calcification extension (Lev disease)
differential_by_imaging.rationale: Aortic valve calcification may extend into the membranous septum and His bundle region

findings.size: Scattered punctate to confluent foci, Fazekas grade 2

output.impression: Brain MRI demonstrates moderate chronic small vessel ischemic disease consistent with the patient's cardiovascular risk factors (hypertension, diabetes, hyperlipidemia). No acute intracranial pathology to explain syncope. No evidence of acute infarction, hemorrhage, or mass. The findings do not support a structural neurological cause for the patient's episodes of loss of consciousness.
recommended_actions: Cardiovascular risk factor optimization recommended for small vessel disease

CT_Head_Report.clinical_significance: Chronic small vessel ischemic disease, consistent with cardiovascular risk factors
Consistent with reported fall, no underlying fracture

output.interpretation: Non-contrast CT head shows no acute intracranial pathology. No hemorrhage, mass, or acute ischemic changes. Moderate chronic small vessel ischemic disease. Left temporal soft tissue swelling from reported fall, without underlying fracture. No intracranial cause for the patient's syncopal episodes identified.
abnormal_values_summary: Left temporal subgaleal swelling (post-traumatic from fall)

ground_truth.primary_diagnosis: Cardiac syncope due to intermittent high-degree atrioventricular block in the setting of trifascicular conduction disease

differential.key_distinguishing: Brief convulsive movements (convulsive syncope) mimic seizure, but duration is only 10-15 seconds, no tonic phase, no post-ictal confusion beyond seconds, no tongue biting, no incontinence, normal EEG. Rapid return to baseline is atypical for seizure.
No identifiable trigger for episodes, no prodrome of nausea/warmth/diaphoresis, age and cardiac conduction disease make cardiac etiology far more likely. Negative tilt table test.

Two of three episodes occurred while seated, not on standing. On antihypertensives and thiazide which could contribute, but positional component is absent. Initial misdiagnosis by PCP addressed this incorrectly.
Structural heart disease and conduction abnormalities could predispose to VT, but Holter showed no VT, low PVC burden, and documented high-degree AV block as the culprit arrhythmia.
Patient has only mild aortic stenosis (valve area 1.5 cm2, mean gradient 15 mmHg), which is insufficient to cause hemodynamically significant obstruction. Episodes not exertion-related.

optimal_actions.expected_finding: Bifascicular block (RBBB + LAFB) with first-degree AV block (trifascicular pattern)
Monitor for intermittent high-degree AV block or pauses

optimal_actions.action: Obtain comprehensive labs including CBC, BMP, troponin, BNP, magnesium, TSH
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Mild hypokalemia and hypomagnesemia (thiazide-related), elevated BNP, normal troponin

optimal_actions.action: Correct electrolyte abnormalities: IV magnesium and potassium supplementation
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Correction of hypokalemia and hypomagnesemia to reduce arrhythmia risk

optimal_actions.action: Obtain EEG to evaluate for epileptiform activity given witnessed convulsive movements
Recognize convulsive syncope as the explanation for witnessed jerking movements, not epilepsy
Urgent cardiology consultation for evaluation of trifascicular block with syncope
Obtain transthoracic echocardiogram to assess for structural heart disease
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Concentric LVH, mildly reduced LVEF, mild aortic stenosis with calcification

optimal_actions.action: Arrange extended cardiac monitoring (Holter or event monitor) to capture intermittent arrhythmia
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Intermittent Mobitz type II block and transient complete heart block

optimal_actions.action: Admit to telemetry unit with transcutaneous pacing pads applied as precaution
Hold hydrochlorothiazide given electrolyte abnormalities exacerbating conduction disease
Plan for permanent dual-chamber (DDD) pacemaker implantation

critical_actions: Obtain 12-lead ECG to identify bifascicular block with first-degree AV block (trifascicular pattern)
Recognize that brief convulsive movements represent convulsive syncope rather than epileptic seizures
Urgent cardiology consultation for trifascicular block with recurrent syncope
Have transcutaneous pacing available given high risk for complete heart block
Admit to monitored setting and plan for permanent pacemaker implantation
contraindicated_actions: Starting antiepileptic drugs based on witnessed convulsive movements without confirmed epilepsy diagnosis
Discharging the patient without cardiac monitoring and pacemaker evaluation
Administering AV-nodal blocking agents (beta-blockers, non-dihydropyridine calcium channel blockers, digoxin)
Performing lumbar puncture (no indication for CSF analysis in this presentation)
Administering IV adenosine (would worsen AV block)
Attributing syncope to orthostatic hypotension without investigating cardiac conduction disease
key_reasoning_points: Convulsive syncope (brief myoclonic jerks from cerebral hypoperfusion during cardiac arrest) is commonly mistaken for epileptic seizures; the key distinction is the brevity of jerking (<30 seconds), absence of tonic phase, and rapid return to full alertness without post-ictal confusion

The trifascicular block pattern on ECG (first-degree AV block + RBBB + LAFB) indicates disease in all three fascicles of the infranodal conduction system, with only the left posterior fascicle partially conducting

Syncope in the setting of trifascicular block is presumed to be due to intermittent complete heart block until proven otherwise and is an indication for pacemaker even without documentation of complete block

The initial misdiagnosis as orthostatic hypotension (first episode while standing) demonstrates anchoring bias; the second and third episodes while seated should have redirected the evaluation to cardiac causes
Hypokalemia and hypomagnesemia from thiazide diuretic use can exacerbate conduction abnormalities and should be corrected, though they are not the primary cause

A normal EEG does not definitively exclude epilepsy, but combined with the clinical features of rapid recovery, no post-ictal state, and cardiac conduction disease on ECG, it strongly supports a cardiac etiology

The ECG finding of bifascicular block is the key moderate-difficulty feature: unlike complete heart block which is immediately obvious, the clinician must recognize that intermittent progression to complete block is the likely mechanism even when not captured on the presenting ECG
Aortic valve calcification extending into the conduction system (Lev disease) is a common mechanism for progressive conduction system disease in older adults

metadata.author: NeuroBench generation pipeline
clinical_pearls: Convulsive syncope occurs in up to 12% of syncopal episodes and is often misdiagnosed as epilepsy, leading to inappropriate antiepileptic drug treatment
Trifascicular block (bifascicular block plus first-degree AV block) with syncope carries a high risk of progression to complete heart block and is a Class I indication for permanent pacemaker
The ECG in cardiac syncope may not capture the culprit arrhythmia at the time of presentation; the resting ECG findings of conduction disease provide critical diagnostic clues
Thiazide diuretics can cause electrolyte abnormalities (hypokalemia, hypomagnesemia) that exacerbate cardiac conduction abnormalities
